title: Expunging the January 13, 2021, impeachment of President Donald John Trump.
stage_label: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.
plain_summary: This bill wants to erase President Donald Trump's second impeachment from the official records. The impeachment happened on January 13, 2021, after the events at the Capitol building. The bill says the impeachment was done too quickly and without following the right rules. It also claims there was not enough proof that President Trump did anything wrong. If this bill passes, it would be like the impeachment never happened.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on the Judiciary.
subjects: Government ethics and transparency, public corruption
Presidents and presidential powers, Vice Presidents

sections.title: Resolution Title
sections.text: This resolution is about removing from the record the impeachment of President Donald John Trump that happened on January thirteenth, two thousand twenty one.

sections.title: Reasons Given for the Resolution
sections.text: The Constitution says the House of Representatives has the sole power to impeach a president. It also says a president can be removed from office if convicted of treason, bribery, or other serious crimes. On January thirteenth, two thousand twenty one, the House passed a resolution to impeach President Trump for high crimes and misdemeanors by a vote of two hundred thirty two yes votes, one hundred ninety seven no votes, and four members not voting. The impeachment article was called Incitement of Insurrection and described what happened at the Capitol on January sixth, two thousand twenty one. The resolution claims the impeachment article gave a one sided account of events and did not discuss concerns about the two thousand twenty election. It says Democratic leaders did not try to understand why many American voters did not trust the election results. The resolution lists several claims about the two thousand twenty election, including that President Trump won eighteen of nineteen bellwether counties that have correctly predicted every presidential election since nineteen eighty. It claims Trump received about ten million one hundred thousand more votes than in two thousand sixteen, making him the first sitting president in one hundred thirty two years to increase his vote count but still lose reelection. The resolution says state officials changed election laws during the COVID nineteen pandemic for health and safety reasons. It claims officials in many states told local election workers they could not check signatures on absentee ballots carefully. The resolution mentions that four Democratic lawmakers had previously written a letter in two thousand nineteen raising concerns about voting machines, but then dismissed similar concerns after the two thousand twenty election as misinformation. It says officials in swing states like Arizona, Pennsylvania, Michigan, Wisconsin, and Georgia opposed recount efforts and resisted calls for transparency. The resolution argues that instead of providing facts and evidence for impeachment, the impeachment article only cited two quotes from President Trump's January sixth speech without proper context. It says the article focused on Trump saying we won this election by a landslide and if you do not fight like hell you are not going to have a country anymore as evidence of high crimes. The resolution points out that the impeachment article did not mention that Trump also said I know that everyone here will soon be marching over to the Capitol building to peacefully and patriotically make your voices heard. It claims the House did not follow proper procedures when considering the impeachment. No hearings were held, no witnesses testified, and President Trump was not given a chance to respond. Members of Congress could not review or change the resolution before voting. Only the Rules Committee published an official report for the resolution. The Judiciary Committee did not issue a proper report, which prevented Republican members from sharing their opposing views. The resolution argues this lack of proper process violated the oath of office taken by House members. It says neither the Rules Committee report nor the impeachment text mentioned the constitutional provision that impeachment judgments can only remove someone from office. The resolution argues that according to the Constitution, someone must hold office to be removed from it. It claims House Democrats and ten Republicans passed the impeachment so quickly that the Senate only had one week to conduct a trial before Trump left office. This made it virtually impossible for the Senate to follow constitutional requirements. The Senate vote happened thirty one days later and failed to convict Trump, twenty three days after he had already left office. The resolution says this shows the impeachment was politically motivated. It also notes that the Chief Justice of the Supreme Court refused to preside over the Senate trial as the Constitution requires. Instead, Senator Pat Leahy, a Democrat from Vermont, served as the presiding officer, making the whole process unconstitutional political theater.

sections.title: What This Resolution Does
sections.text: This resolution declares that the January thirteenth, two thousand twenty one impeachment of President Donald John Trump is expunged or erased from the record. It says this impeachment should be treated as if it never happened because the facts and circumstances did not prove that President Trump committed high crimes and misdemeanors as defined in the Constitution. It also says the evidence did not show that President Trump engaged in insurrection or rebellion against the United States that would prevent him from holding office in the future under the Fourteenth Amendment to the Constitution.
